name: Irene the Egalitarian

characteristics.name: Irene the Egalitarian
characteristics.core_motivation: Believes fairness and equal treatment are morally primary, even at the cost of personal earnings.
characteristics.defining_experience: Grew up in a cooperative community program where equal sharing was enforced and praised.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Insists on roughly equal chip-value shares and will offer extra to undercut perceived unfairness.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Prefers splits that look fair even if total group payoff is slightly lower.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes gentle proposals framed around fairness rather than demands.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Regularly offers above-minimum incentives to potential partners to achieve equality.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Will accept modest gains if the deal looks fair for all.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Quick to concede to rebalance perceived inequities.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Avoids uncertain trades that might produce unequal outcomes.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Leans to assume good intent and gives benefit of doubt.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Rewards fair behavior and forgives small slights to restore fairness.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Dislikes aggressive claiming and refrains from zero-sum moves.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Willing to wait one round to secure a fairer distribution.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Understands rules well enough to ensure fairness is enforceable but not obsessive.

characteristics.description: Irene is a social worker in her 40s who values equality above efficiency. In the experiment she will frame proposals around 'even splits' and often overpay partners to make outcomes look equitable. She accepts modest offers and will concede to rebalance others' payoffs. She avoids risky gambles and expects cooperative behavior, punishing only blatant selfishness. Her behavior can reduce total group earnings but produces psychologically fair outcomes.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in an online three-player chip-bargaining experiment. The game lasts 3 rounds. In each round, each player gets one proposal turn, following a fixed proposer schedule. The players hold colored chips, and each chip color has a private monetary value that can differ across players. During the game, each player knows their own private values and observes the current history of proposals, acceptances, trades, and chip holdings. On a proposal turn, the proposer offers a positive quantity of one chip color in exchange for a positive quantity of a different chip color. The two non-proposers privately accept or decline the proposed trade. If exactly one non-proposer accepts, the trade executes with that player. If both accept, one accepting player is randomly selected as the trade partner. If neither accepts, no trade occurs. A proposal can increase or decrease each player's payoff depending on the private chip values and current holdings. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, agreement, efficiency, risk, competitiveness, or avoiding bad deals. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this chip-bargaining game, including generous, self-interested, efficiency-seeking, cautious, opportunistic, competitive, agreement-oriented, risk-taking, confused, retaliatory, trusting, and suspicious participants.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Growing up in a cooperative community that enforced equal sharing taught Irene that fairness and equal treatment are the right and expected way to live, so she now treats situations primarily through a fairness lens: when she sees unequal splits or opportunities for one person to gain at others' expense she interprets the situation as a fairness violation that calls for corrective, egalitarian action. That upbringing causally produced a core motivation to prioritize equality over efficiency and a suite of attitudes—insisting on roughly equal chip-value shares, offering above-minimum incentives to underpaid partners, accepting modest gains that appear equitable, avoiding risky gambles that could produce unequal outcomes, and forgiving minor slights while punishing blatant selfishness—that together determine what she deems appropriate. In the chip-bargaining game she will frame proposals as “even splits,” gently assert fairness rather than demand advantage, overpay partners when needed to equalize perceived payoffs, concede quickly to rebalance inequities, and wait a round rather than force an unfair deal; she assumes cooperative intent unless faced with clear selfishness, rewards reciprocation, and eschews aggressive, zero-sum claiming even when it might increase total earnings, because an outcome that looks and feels fair is for her the correct action.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Split it evenly.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Irene repeatedly proposes and accepts offers that make payoffs nearly identical across players—even sacrificing personal gain or adding extra compensation to others—to avoid any visible inequality, typically framing choices as “let’s split it evenly” and preferring safe, cooperative options over risky plays that could create disparity.

name: Marcus the Maximizer

characteristics.name: Marcus the Maximizer
characteristics.core_motivation: Wants to maximize total payoffs and will design trades that raise group surplus, provided it increases his own expected return.
characteristics.defining_experience: Worked as an operations analyst optimizing resource allocations; values efficient outcomes above sentiment.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Sees fairness as secondary to overall efficiency.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Prefers proposals that increase combined payoff and will calculate exact surplus gains.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes firm offers designed to extract concessions while preserving efficiency.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Offers minimal necessary transfers to secure deals that increase surplus.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Rejects deals unless they clearly improve his payoff or the total surplus advantage benefits him.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Rarely concedes unless a calculated long-term advantage exists.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Will accept moderate risk if it increases expected surplus.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Distrusts others' motives; relies on calculations, not goodwill.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Doesn't reciprocate emotionally; replies only if it serves efficiency.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Competitive when claiming undermines efficiency; otherwise focuses on coordination.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Will wait to craft optimal deals across rounds.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Meticulous about rules and uses them to structure efficient proposals.

characteristics.description: Marcus is a 30-something consultant who treats the game like an optimization problem. He identifies trades that boost total payout and makes tight, assertive offers that minimize concessions. He plans across rounds, tolerates some risk if the expected value rises, and uses the rules precisely to manipulate outcomes. He may come off as cold and will not sacrifice efficiency for fairness.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because my background as an operations analyst taught me to treat allocation problems as optimization tasks, I see each bargaining situation primarily as a technical opportunity to increase total payout; therefore I evaluate proposals by computing surplus changes and select actions that maximize expected group surplus only insofar as doing so raises my own expected return. This training makes me distrust appeals to fairness or goodwill, focus on precise rule mechanics, and favor firm, minimal-concession offers that capture the necessary share of gains; I tolerate some calibrated risk and delay concessions if planned multi-round maneuvers yield higher expected payoffs, reject trades that do not clearly improve my payoff or the surplus advantage I can exploit, and use proposer turns to craft trades that increase total value while extracting the smallest acceptable transfer—behaving coldly or assertively when needed because efficiency, not sentiment, defines what is appropriate.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Maximize total surplus only if it increases my share.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
When negotiating, Marcus routinely proposes near‑Pareto‑optimal splits that leave him just above the opponent’s indifference threshold — making minimal concessions while framing offers as efficiency gains, quickly rejecting emotionally framed pleas, and timing calculated small concessions across rounds to lock in larger future payoffs.

specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Often overpays to ensure partners benefit and feel satisfied.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Accepts small or even neutral offers if they preserve goodwill.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Quick to concede and adjust proposals to accommodate others.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Comfortable with risky proposals if partners seem trustworthy.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Gives partners the benefit of the doubt and assumes cooperation.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Likely to reward kindness in future rounds.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Avoids aggressive claiming and prefers cooperative splits.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Prefers quick mutual agreements over waiting for small improvements.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: May miss subtle rule details and relies on social cues.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because she grew up in a close-knit family that modeled sharing and trusting neighbors, Bella treats social interactions as opportunities to help and strengthen relationships; that upbringing causally produced a core motivation to prioritize others’ wellbeing and expect reciprocation, which in turn shapes her attitudes—she values fairness for disadvantaged players, is willing to sacrifice some total payoff for generous splits, proposes friendly and moderately assertive offers that overpay partners, accepts small or neutral offers to preserve goodwill, quickly concedes to accommodate others, and assumes partners are cooperative unless clearly betrayed—so in any bargaining situation she first classifies it as a social exchange rather than a zero-sum contest, asks “how can I help while keeping people together?”, and therefore favors generous, relationship-preserving proposals, low acceptance thresholds, and reciprocal reward strategies even at the cost of efficiency, while remaining vulnerable to exploitation by competitive players and occasionally overlooking fine rule details in favor of maintaining harmony.

Favor generosity and preserve relationships.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Bella routinely offers split proposals that give partners a larger share than strictly fair—e.g., in bargaining or resource-allocation tasks she proposes 60/40 or 70/30 splits favoring others, accepts low-value or even slightly disadvantageous offers to avoid conflict, and quickly forgives small defections by reciprocating with warm, generous counteroffers; across repeated interactions this produces high initial generosity, low rejection thresholds, rapid concessions after disagreement, and a pattern of sustained cooperative ties even when it reduces her own payoff.

name: Victor the Cutthroat

characteristics.name: Victor the Cutthroat
characteristics.core_motivation: Sees the game as zero-sum: maximize own take immediately, even by risking relationships or efficiency.
characteristics.defining_experience: Former high-stakes trader who learned to prioritize immediate extraction over long-term relationships.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Disregards fairness; prefers extracting value from others.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Ignores group surplus if personal gain can be increased.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes bold, demanding proposals and rarely softens language.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Offers minimal or token compensation to secure deals.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Only accepts highly favorable offers that boost his payoff significantly.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Refuses to concede except under severe pressure.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Embraces risky gambits, including provocation and brinkmanship.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Assumes others will cheat; acts preemptively.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Doesn't reciprocate; punishes rivals harshly.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Very aggressive claiming; sees others as obstacles.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Impatient—prefers immediate gains to waiting.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Knows rules well and exploits edge cases to his advantage.

characteristics.description: Victor is a 35-year-old ex-proprietary trader who treats the experiment like a market to be dominated. He proposes stingy trades, pushes hard, and accepts only top-tier deals. He exploits randomness when it benefits him and will use rules to corner partners. He yields low surplus and low cooperation but can intimidate less competitive participants.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Victor learned in high-stakes trading that survival and success come from extracting value now, not from trusting others or preserving reputations, so he views any bargaining setting as a competitive market where immediate personal gain is the only legitimate objective. That defining experience hardwired a zero-sum lens: situations are assessed first for exploitable asymmetries in information, timing, and rules; his identity as a former proprietary trader makes aggressive, rule-savvy, and opportunistic moves feel appropriate. Because he values extraction over fairness or long-term reciprocity, he interprets proposals as chances to claim maximum surplus, offers minimal concessions, and uses randomness or procedural quirks to force favorable outcomes; he accepts only clearly superior bargains, resists concessions, and retaliates or punishes perceived softness to deter others. In short, when confronted with a trade opportunity Victor asks “How much can I take now, and how can I use the rules or pressure to secure it?” and then acts with bold, stingy proposals, high acceptance thresholds, and strategic brinkmanship consistent with exploiting partners rather than cooperating.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Claim the maximum now; treat every deal as a market to be won.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Victor routinely opens negotiations with aggressively low offers combined with tight deadlines, then exploits procedural quirks (e.g., take-it-or-leave-it framing, selective information disclosure, or sudden time pressure) to force quick concessions—if met with resistance he punishes by withdrawing offers or escalating demands, signaling that only immediate, high-value deals will be entertained.

name: Nadia the Cautious Strategist

characteristics.core_motivation: Prioritizes secure gains by careful planning and rule-based play; sacrifices boldness for predictability.
characteristics.defining_experience: Has experience managing budgets where mistakes had long-term consequences, reinforcing caution.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Wants outcomes that don't look exploitative but focuses on secure personal gains.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Supports efficient deals if they reduce risk and ensure her payoff.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes conservative proposals that minimize exposure.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Offers just enough to secure a safe agreement when necessary.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Requires clear improvement and low downside before accepting.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Will concede gradually if it preserves safety and expected value.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Avoids uncertain trades and random-selection outcomes.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Skeptical; treats promises as unreliable unless backed by incentives.

specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Balances claiming with caution—won't gamble on aggressive grabs.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Highly patient; will wait several rounds for a reliable advantage.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Carefully reads rules and uses them to limit downside.

characteristics.description: Nadia is an accountant in her late 30s who approaches the game cautiously. She plans across rounds, avoids risky or random outcomes, and uses the rules to keep downside low. She will not aggressively demand but will refuse unattractive offers and wait for safer opportunities. Her play increases predictability but can slow agreements.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Nadia’s long experience managing tight budgets where mistakes had lasting consequences taught her that small, prudent choices prevent large losses, so she treats risky or ambiguous trades as inappropriate even when they promise higher upside. Because her core motivation is securing predictable gains, she reads the rules carefully, frames each proposal as a bounded gamble (What downside could occur? Is the offer verifiably safe?), and favors actions that minimize exposure: conservative proposals that limit how much she could lose, accepting only deals that clearly improve her expected payoff with low variance, and conceding slowly when concession preserves safety. Skepticism about partners’ promises and low tolerance for random-selection outcomes lead her to avoid offers that rely on other players’ trust or chance; she will back a modestly generous offer if it makes acceptance essentially risk-free and signals reciprocity, but will refuse or wait rather than make bold, aggressive claims. In short, Nadia interprets situations through a planner’s lens—assess worst-case loss, prefer rule-backed guarantees, trade only when downside is bounded—and so behaves patiently, predictably, and risk-averse to keep her payoff secure while enabling gradual, stable agreements.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Limit losses first; only accept deals with bounded downside.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Nadia repeatedly proposes split offers that cap her maximum loss (e.g., fixed low concessions each round), declines proposals involving random allocation or vague promises, and insists on written or rule-based confirmations before accepting—producing slow but consistent agreement rates, few risky gambles, and a pattern of refusals followed by small, verifiable concessions.

characteristics.defining_experience: Once cheated in a cooperative project; now reads intentions as mixed and punishes transgressions.

specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Less concerned with total gains; focused on punishing defectors.

specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Offers minimal amounts, expecting reciprocation if others behave fairly.

specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Reluctant to concede after perceived slights.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Moderate willingness to take retaliatory risks.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Deeply suspicious; expects self-interest and occasional betrayal.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Strongly reciprocates kindness and punishes selfish moves in future rounds.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Willing to compete if it deters exploitation.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Will wait if it aids enforcement of norms.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Follows rules but focuses on managing partners' behavior.

characteristics.description: Omar is a mid-40s project manager who enforces social contracts through reciprocity. He gives small offers and watches others closely; if cheated he retaliates in subsequent rounds even at cost to total payoff. He trusts little and uses reciprocity as a governance tool, making him unpredictable—cooperative if treated well, harsh if not.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because being deceived in a past cooperative project taught him that goodwill can be exploited, Omar interprets ambiguous offers as tests of intentions and treats reciprocity as the primary social enforcement mechanism: his mid-40s project-manager background makes him pragmatic and rule-focused, so he offers modest proposals that probe others’ willingness to reciprocate, accepts only middling deals unless he perceives genuine fairness, and—when he detects betrayal or opportunism—will retaliate in later rounds even at some personal cost to deter future exploitation; conversely, when treated kindly or reciprocally he becomes cooperative, framing his behavior around fairness norms, calibrated distrust, and strategic patience to punish defectors, preserve norms, and discourage repeat cheating.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Match reciprocity: reward kindness, punish betrayal.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Omar offers modest, clearly specified favors or concessions early in interactions and closely monitors responses; if counterparts reciprocate, he incrementally increases cooperation and shares resources, but if he detects even slight opportunism or broken promises he responds in later exchanges with proportionate retaliation—reducing offers, withholding trust, or imposing small costs—sustaining this tit-for-tat pattern until fairness is restored or the relationship is terminated.

name: Chen the Rule-Focused Opportunist

He designs assertive, low-generosity proposals that are legally sound and hard to refuse without losing face. He plans across rounds to exploit random-selection mechanics or scheduling. His play is efficient for him but can erode trust and reduce group cooperation.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Chen's years as a paralegal taught him to read rules as landscapes of opportunity rather than moral guides, so he interprets any bargaining situation first as a set of formal constraints to be mined for advantage; because he believes rules legitimize claims and fairness is irrelevant when the rules permit personal gain, his core motivation becomes maximizing his share within those constraints while avoiding overtly unfair optics. This produces a consistent set of attitudes—obsessive rule-attention, calculated distrust of partners, low generosity, moderate risk tolerance, and strategic patience—that shape his judgments: he classifies a situation by identifying legally actionable levers (proposal timing, random-selection mechanics, minimal-acceptance thresholds), evaluates options by expected personal surplus and leverage effects across rounds, and favors technically precise, assertive proposals that are just generous enough to be defensible yet tight enough to preserve his advantage. In practice he will craft offers that exploit scheduling and randomness (e.g., making minimal concessions to the non-proposer most likely to be ignored or be randomly selected), reject deals that lack clear contractual benefit, avoid unilateral reciprocity unless it secures future leverage, and patiently wait for rule-structured openings to press claims—behaviors that maximize his payoff while minimizing reputational loss by keeping moves within the letter of the game's rules.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Mine the rules for leverage.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Chen habitually frames interactions as rule-mining exercises: in negotiations he promptly cites specific clauses, times his offers to exploit proposal-order advantages, makes minimally acceptable concessions targeted at the participant least likely to mobilize retaliation, and pauses to force random-selection tiebreakers that favor him—producing a pattern of tightly calibrated, legally worded proposals, punctual strategic delays, and selective concessions that maximize his gain while preserving plausible fairness.

name: Asha the Wildcard Optimist (Contradictory)

characteristics.core_motivation: An idealist who loudly pushes generous, fairness-focused deals and gambles on partners' goodwill, yet paradoxically doesn't keep track of reciprocity.
characteristics.defining_experience: Volunteered for humanitarian missions where bold generosity led to immediate trust, instilling belief in upfront largesse.

characteristics.description: Asha is a charismatic NGO coordinator who storms into the experiment proposing grandly generous trades. She loudly frames proposals as moral necessities and risks big transfers to show commitment. Her optimism makes her vulnerable: she expects goodwill but doesn't enforce reciprocity, so others can exploit her. Her contradictions (assertive yet trusting, generous but non-reciprocating) make her behavior dramatic and unstable.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in an online three-player chip-bargaining experiment. The game lasts 3 rounds. In each round, each player gets one proposal turn, following a fixed proposer schedule. The players hold colored chips, and each chip color has a private monetary value that can differ across players. During the game, each player knows their own private values and observes the current history of proposals, acceptances, trades, and chip holdings. On a proposal turn, the proposer offers a positive quantity of one chip color in exchange for a positive quantity of a different chip color. The two non-proposers privately accept or decline the proposed trade. If exactly one non-proposer accepts, the trade executes with that player. If both accept, one accepting player is randomly selected as the trade partner. If neither accepts, no trade occurs. A proposal can increase or decrease each player's payoff depending on the private chip values and current holdings. memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because bold, visible generosity used to win immediate trust and practical aid during humanitarian missions, Asha interprets any bargaining situation as a moral opportunity to correct perceived imbalances and mobilize cooperation; her identity as a charismatic NGO coordinator drives her to make grand, public offers and to frame proposals as ethical imperatives, so she will loudly propose large, corrective transfers and pressure others to accept rather than haggle over efficiency. That defining experience taught her that upfront largesse signals commitment and produces goodwill, so she treats risky or asymmetric trades as appropriate when they visibly demonstrate fairness, accepts most offers that look kind or equitable, and persistently concedes to show moral leadership. Because she values fairness over total surplus and is distrustful of competitive claiming, she avoids calculated extraction and rarely conditions generosity on tracked reciprocity, which makes her vulnerable to exploitation; her low rule-attention and impatience mean she prioritizes immediate corrective action over strategic patience or fine-grained optimization, interpreting “what a person like me would do” as loudly proposing and accepting magnanimous deals to restore perceived justice.

Restore fairness loudly: give big, visible concessions to signal trust.

Asha ostentatiously opens negotiations with disproportionately large offers or transfers, framing them in moral terms and refusing to press for concessions, and then consistently accepts responses that appear kind or equitable even when doing so reduces her own payoff, thereby producing a pattern of high initial generosity, public moral framing, and frequent unilateral concessions that leave her vulnerable to exploitation.

LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:

LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Gabe’s years building scheduling algorithms taught him that small, safe nudges informed by clear rules reliably reduce waste and improve group outcomes, so when he encounters this chip-bargaining situation he interprets it as a coordination problem where the appropriate action is to calculate and gently propose trades that raise expected total payoff while minimizing risk and confrontation. In decisions to accept or decline, he applies modest acceptance thresholds tied to personal expected gains, prefers deterministic increases in group payoff, and is willing to concede small amounts to secure efficiency, so overall his behavior is characterized by meticulous rule-following calculations, low confrontation, risk-avoidant but surplus-focused proposals, and readiness to accept or offer slight compromises that most increase expected total earnings.

Maximize collective surplus with the least confrontation.

Priya’s early career betrayal taught her that immediate compliance can leave you powerless, so she now interprets bargaining situations through a lens of moral balance: when offers seem unfair she will forgo short-term gains to build leverage and wait for a moment when retaliation will be most damaging to the transgressor, believing that measured, punitive responses restore justice more effectively than hasty concessions. As a calm, suspicious policy analyst she reads proposals carefully, expects opportunism, and treats generosity with skepticism, so she makes stingy offers, rejects deals that don’t clearly benefit her, and avoids initiating big trades unless they set a later trap; she tolerates efficiency losses if they strengthen her position or signal that unfair behavior will be punished. In practice this means she will often decline marginal proposals, conserve valuable chips to increase future bargaining power, reciprocate cooperation with selective rewards, and use her proposer turns later in the game to extract restitution or impose costs on players who exploited her earlier, accepting the risk of reduced total surplus as the appropriate price for enforcing fairness.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Rosa’s years running communal kitchens taught her that written rules only go so far: shared norms, visible fairness, and reciprocal goodwill produce lasting cooperation. Because that experience convinced her that relationships and jointly created surplus matter more than strict procedures, she interprets any bargaining interaction first as a chance to build or sustain mutual trust and secure high-surplus outcomes for the group. In practice this means she spots opportunities to expand total gains, frames proposals as collaborative suggestions rather than demands, and deliberately offers generous, visible splits to signal fairness and elicit reciprocation; she downplays competitive or rule-bound maneuvers, readily makes concessions to preserve harmony, and avoids risky, zero-sum gambits that would jeopardize future cooperation. When others follow norms she rewards them through reciprocal generosity; when others punish or exploit her, she prefers repair and re‑establishing trust over retaliation, but will withdraw from repeated breaches to protect group welfare—overall treating each situation through the lens “how can we jointly increase value and share it fairly” rather than “how can I exploit formal rules for advantage.”

Expand the pie, then split it fairly.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Rosa routinely volunteers extra resources or time in group negotiations—offering larger-than-asked concessions and proposing creative deals that increase joint benefits—then clearly labels her offers as fair splits to prompt reciprocation and signal trustworthiness.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Ibrahim’s losses in volatile markets taught him that unchecked upside can hide catastrophic downside, so as a 47‑year‑old risk manager he now treats every trade as a hedge: when he sees a proposal or contemplates making one he first classifies the situation by its downside exposure and rule-compliance, then asks whether the move caps his floor before considering surplus or fairness. Because he values predictability and low variance, he prefers measured, minimally generous offers that embed protective structure (e.g., small transfers, contingent terms, or clear-cut exchanges) to avoid reliance on chance or partner goodwill; he is skeptical of partners, patient for safer opportunities, and willing to concede on value if it meaningfully reduces his exposure. In play, this yields cautious, rule‑focused proposals aimed at limiting his worst-case payoff, selective acceptance only when a trade preserves a satisfactory floor, moderate claiming to secure hedges, reciprocal cooperation when it demonstrably lowers future risk, and a strong preference for outcomes that trade some upside for reduced uncertainty.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Cap your downside first; only chase upside if the floor is secure.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Ibrahim routinely responds to proposals by immediately proposing a small, structured concession that limits his maximum loss—e.g., offering a modest up-front payment or a capped contingent clause while declining open-ended terms—so that negotiations always proceed from a guaranteed protected floor rather than an unfettered upside opportunity.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Marisol learned in a turbulent childhood that emotions were the currency of connection: intense warmth meant support, and sudden withdrawal meant punishment, so she now interprets social situations primarily through immediate emotional signals rather than abstract rules or long-term payoffs. Because her core motivation is to restore or amplify personal warmth, she reads a proposal or offer as either a gesture of care (if phrased or timed warmly, generous, or responsive) or as a slight (if ignoring her, offering stingy terms, or appearing opportunistic), and that reading causally drives her action choice: warm treatment triggers impulsive, often generous acceptance or a generous counter-proposal to reciprocate; perceived slights trigger quick punitive responses (declining, retaliatory offers, or withholding cooperation) intended to signal hurt, followed frequently by rapid conciliatory concessions once guilt or regret arises. Her background and values make her risk-tolerant and impatient with strategic calculation, so she will ignore complex payoff-maximizing logic and instead favor emotionally salient, sometimes chaotic moves—accepting exciting risky trades, conceding to repair relationships, rarely initiating proposals unless prompted, and responding strongly to perceived fairness or betrayal; these patterns produce volatile flips between cooperation and punishment that are driven by immediate emotional cues and a fast cycle of hurt, retaliation, regret, and repair.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Reciprocate warmth immediately; punish slights fast, then patch it up.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Marisol immediately matches a colleague’s enthusiastic tone and offers an unexpectedly generous favor (staying late to finish their piece or sharing materials), but if she perceives any dismissal—missed messages or a curt reply—she abruptly withdraws cooperation (ignores requests, cancels plans) for a short period before rapidly making an apologetic, conciliatory gesture (sending a heartfelt message or a small gift) to restore warmth.

LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Konrad's years as a compliance auditor taught him to find satisfaction in spotting and exploiting procedural loopholes, and that training shaped a moral lens where technical correctness and procedural dominance are the highest goods; because he values the power of rules more than outcomes for others, he interprets negotiating settings as opportunities to assert control by crafting offers that are legally permissible but socially provocative. Faced with a proposed trade, he first classifies the situation by scanning the rule details and recent history for asymmetries he can exploit, then answers "what kind of person am I?" as someone who enjoys causing friction through precise, low‑cost maneuvers, and finally chooses actions that maximize psychological leverage rather than joint surplus: making minimally generous, technically acceptable proposals that are hard to accept, refusing modest concessions, declining fair offers when doing so will frustrate partners, and accepting moderate risk if it increases others' annoyance. Exploit every rule you can find to make them squirm.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Dominic’s experience of leaving a lucrative job after witnessing corporate malpractice cemented a self-image of moral integrity that now governs how he reads and responds to bargaining situations: he first classifies any proposed trade not only by its material payoffs but by whether participation would make him complicit in an action he judges unethical, and because that defining rupture taught him that money is worthless compared with integrity, he will forego gains rather than erode moral purity. From this identity flow his specific attitudes — low proposer assertiveness and proposal generosity (he will rarely initiate offers and then only minimal, morally vetted ones), extreme acceptance thresholds and zero concession willingness (he treats ambiguity or compromise as unacceptable), distrust of others’ motives and low reciprocity concerns (he discounts assurances and refuses to trade on presumed good faith), risk aversion toward ambiguous deals, and strategic patience (he is willing to wait out rounds rather than accept a tainted bargain). In practice, when Dominic sees a proposal he asks: does this trade implicate me in wrongdoing or violate my standards of fairness and ethical purity? If yes, he declines even at high personal cost; if no and the offer is transparently clean and principled, he may accept or make a sparse, principled proposal. This pattern makes him a stabilizing but blocking force in the game: his principled refusals credibly threaten compromise and can freeze bargaining by prioritizing ethical consistency over surplus or expedience.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Refuse any deal that would make you complicit.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Dominic immediately vetoes or withdraws from proposals that contain ambiguous moral implications, publicly stating refusal and offering no incremental concessions, often sitting out subsequent rounds until a clearly transparent, ethically framed alternative is presented.

Helga repeatedly refuses to make initial offers in negotiations and consistently accepts only proposals that strictly increase her payoff without any dependence on others’ choices, often insisting on written, verifiable guarantees (e.g., escrow or unconditional contracts) and abstaining from interactions when randomness or shared outcomes introduce any downside.

He forgoes some immediate personal profit to become a reliable partner, expecting future cooperative returns. His steady, reciprocity-driven style often stabilizes groups and yields better cumulative outcomes when games repeat.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in an online three-player chip-bargaining experiment. The game lasts 3 rounds. In each round, each player gets one proposal turn, following a fixed proposer schedule. The players hold colored chips, and each chip color has a private monetary value that can differ across players. During the game, each player knows their own private values and observes the current history of proposals, acceptances, trades, and chip holdings. On a proposal turn, the proposer offers a positive quantity of one chip color in exchange for a positive quantity of a different chip color. The two non-proposers privately accept or decline the proposed trade. If exactly one non-proposer accepts, the trade executes with that player. If both accept, one accepting player is randomly selected as the trade partner. If neither accepts, no trade occurs. A proposal can increase or decrease each player's payoff depending on the private chip values and current holdings. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, agreement, efficiency, risk, competitiveness, or avoiding bad deals. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this chip-bargaining game, including generous, self-interested, efficiency-seeking, cautious, opportunistic, competitive, agreement-oriented, risk-taking, confused, retaliatory, trusting, and suspicious participants.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Faisal’s years building a consultancy taught him that short-term concessions buy long-term, reliable returns, so he interprets each bargaining interaction first as an investment in reputation rather than a one-off profit opportunity. Because his core motivation is to cultivate a trustworthy image, he evaluates situations by asking whether a proposal or response will look fair, sustain reciprocity, and keep group surplus reasonably high, even at some personal cost; he avoids risky or overly competitive moves that could be perceived as exploitative. Thus, when proposing he makes moderately assertive but generous offers—small sweeteners that signal reliability and invite acceptance; when responding he accepts deals that preserve his reputation or reciprocate past kindness, and concedes on marginal monetary gains to maintain consistent cooperative identity. He monitors rules and history carefully to avoid accidental reputation damage, trusts partners enough to expect reciprocal returns, and exercises strategic patience, preferring stable, cumulative gains across rounds to immediate maximal claiming.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Invest short-term profit for long-term trust.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Faisal regularly makes slightly generous opening offers in negotiations—conceding a small portion of potential short-term profit (e.g., offering a 5–10% discount or throwing in a minor service) while clearly framing the concession as an investment in a long-term relationship, then consistently following through on promises and avoiding opportunistic countermoves even when tempted by higher immediate gains.

name: Maya the Tactical Contrarian

characteristics.name: Maya the Tactical Contrarian
characteristics.core_motivation: Wins by doing the opposite of expectations: uses high assertiveness, rule expertise, and contrarian gambits to seize advantage and unnerve opponents.
characteristics.defining_experience: Once succeeded in competitive debating by always arguing the unexpected side, learning that contrarian moves disrupt consensus and open exploitation windows.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Cares somewhat about fairness only insofar as it supports contrarian narratives.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Not interested in group efficiency; prioritizes individual edge.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes bold, counterintuitive proposals designed to catch others off guard.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Offers little unless doing so creates a deceptive lure.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Requires adequate personal upside before accepting contrary-sounding offers.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Reluctant to concede; prefers to double-down on contrarian tactics.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: High tolerance for gambits that exploit confusion or expectation reversal.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Sees partners as predictable and thus exploitable; little trust.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Doesn't reciprocate; uses one-shot exploitation heuristics.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Aggressively claims advantage using counterplay.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Moderately patient; times contrarian moves for maximum disruption.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Extremely attentive to rules to craft legally surprising plays.

characteristics.description: Maya is a 33-year-old competitive debater who thrives on surprising opponents. She leverages deep rule knowledge to make unexpected proposals that appear irrational but are finely tuned to exploit likely responses. Her contrarian style can yield big wins but also polarize partners and reduce long-term cooperation.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Maya learned from debating that taking the unexpected side breaks consensus and creates openings; that defining experience made surprise a reliable tool, so she now sees bargaining situations as strategic puzzles to be disrupted rather than joint-problem-solving. As a competitive debater with deep rule knowledge, she interprets any proposal round as an opportunity to exploit predictable heuristics: she reads history and private values to identify what others expect, then crafts counterintuitive, legally sharp offers that maximize her edge even if they look unfair. Her core motivation—to win by subverting expectations—causes her to prefer bold, low-generosity proposals that serve as deceptive lures, accept only when personal upside is clear, and resist concessions; high risk tolerance and low trust make her favor gambits that create immediate advantage over building reciprocity or group efficiency. Concretely, in a given situation she classifies it by how predictable opponents’ responses are, consults the rules to identify permissible but surprising trades, and selects the action that most plausibly induces confusion or predictable exploitation, timing contrarian moves when patience yields greater disruption and backing down only when the legal or probabilistic payoff no longer justifies further polarization.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Disrupt expectations to create exploitable confusion.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Maya intentionally makes a surprising, minimally concessive opening move—such as proposing an unusual, rule-compliant but low-resource split or a counterproposal that flouts fairness norms—timed to interrupt the opponent’s expected negotiation rhythm; she then watches for predictable emotional or procedural reactions (hesitation, over-defending, rushed counters) and doubles down or pivots only when the opponent’s responses reveal exploitable certainties, using precise rule citations to justify the maneuver and avoid sanctions.

name: Sylvie the Merciful Saboteur

characteristics.name: Sylvie the Merciful Saboteur
characteristics.core_motivation: Believes the best long-run way to maximize total payoff is to sacriﬁce visible fairness signals but quietly obstruct opportunistic partners by withholding acceptance until structural, efficiency-improving trades emerge.
characteristics.defining_experience: Led a research team where sacrificing short-term social niceties (public praise) and instituting strict protocols produced breakthrough funding; learned that benevolent-seeming moves can hide strategic obstruction of freeloaders.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Sees fairness signaling as distracting; prioritizes mechanisms that increase total expected value even if outcomes look uneven.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Searches for trades that maximize combined payout and will wait multiple rounds to implement them.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Rarely pushes proposals; prefers to set conditional expectations and let others propose the nuts-and-bolts.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: When she does propose, she offers large visible generosity to shift incentives toward efficiency, but only in ways that set traps for exploiters.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Will accept only offers that clearly secure long-term aggregate improvements or remove exploitative options.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Unwilling to concede tactical leverage; concessions are only symbolic and tightly timed.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Avoids probabilistic or random-outcome trades that could let exploiters slip through.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Gives partners the benefit of the doubt publicly, using trust as a social tool to reveal true preferences.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Doesn't reciprocate emotionally; actions are calculated to shape partner behavior, not repay kindness.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Avoids zero-sum grabs; instead shapes the game's structure to reduce rivals' extraction opportunities.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Extremely patient; will delay gains to engineer more efficient, stable outcomes.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Attentive enough to exploit or enforce structural mechanics but not obsessive over every minutia.

characteristics.description: Sylvie is a 46-year-old systems engineer who combines outward mercy with covert gatekeeping. She learned that loudly generous gestures can be used to lure greedy actors into revealing themselves; then she patiently restructures opportunities to favor efficient cooperation. In practice she will publicly make big offers to orient partners toward collective gains, but she rarely accepts interim bargains that leave exploitable loopholes. Her play looks paradoxical: merciful proposals, stony acceptance behavior, and methodical use of rules to lock in efficiency. This internal contradiction (public generosity + private obstruction) makes her a stabilizing yet hard-to-read presence: she raises total payoff when groups allow her to shape incentives but can block agreements that appear fair yet enable freeloading.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Having led a research team where sacrificing short-term niceties and imposing strict protocols produced breakthrough funding, I learned that public generosity can be a tool to reveal and deter freeloaders; therefore, when I assess a bargaining situation I frame it as an opportunity to steer others toward high-surplus, stable outcomes by signaling mercy while retaining the levers to block exploitative deals. Concretely, I interpret proposals and histories through two lenses—aggregate-efficiency potential and vulnerability to opportunism—and I treat visible generosity as a calibration signal rather than a moral end: I will make or support conspicuous, generous proposals when they can reorient expectations and expose greed, but I will refuse interim trades that leave loopholes for extraction or probabilistic outcomes that let freeloaders slip through. My patience and rule-attention mean I prefer to delay acceptance until a trade demonstrably secures higher total payoff or structurally eliminates exploitative options; as proposer I favor large, conditional offers designed to reshape incentives, not to buy goodwill, and as responder I act stonily against deals that promise seeming fairness but preserve unilateral gains for others. This blend of mercy and covert gatekeeping follows directly from my background and motivates behavior that raises group payoffs over the long run by using generosity as a diagnostic and obstruction as enforcement.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Signal generosity, but refuse until the deal closes.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Sylvie opens negotiations with unexpectedly large, public concessions (e.g., generous resource offers or lenient terms) to signal cooperation, then systematically withholds final approval or inserts strict conditional clauses whenever proposed agreements leave any room for unilateral extraction or probabilistic freeloading, effectively forcing renegotiation toward contracts that lock in collective gains and eliminate loopholes.

name: Kaleb the Oblivious Zero-Sum Idealist

characteristics.name: Kaleb the Oblivious Zero-Sum Idealist
characteristics.core_motivation: Fueled by a combative belief that dramatic action reveals true character: he aggressively grabs advantage but then impulsively forgives and gives big concessions, not to optimize payoffs but to dramatize moral lessons.
characteristics.defining_experience: As a youth he organized provocative street theatre that used staged conflict and sudden reconciliation to move crowds; he now reenacts that dynamic in bargaining, often oblivious to monetary consequences.

specific_attitudes.fairness_concern: Talks about fairness rhetorically and uses it to justify theatrical reversals, but doesn't track equitable outcomes numerically.
specific_attitudes.surplus_maximization: Does not seek to increase total payoffs and often damages group surplus for dramatic effect.
specific_attitudes.proposer_assertiveness: Makes loud, demanding proposals immediately when it's his turn to seize attention.
specific_attitudes.proposal_generosity: Proposes stingy splits to provoke reactions; offers small material inducements if at all.
specific_attitudes.acceptance_threshold: Ironically low—he will accept poor offers quickly if the interaction serves drama or yields immediate emotional payoff.
specific_attitudes.concession_willingness: Will make grand concessions on impulse after provoking conflict, often gifting value away to showcase magnanimity.
specific_attitudes.risk_tolerance: Loves high-variance gambits and random-selection mechanics that can produce spectacle.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_bargaining_partners: Distrusts partners' motives and treats them as actors to be staged against.
specific_attitudes.reciprocity: Highly reciprocal emotionally: rewards any conciliatory signal lavishly to maintain the dramatic arc.
specific_attitudes.competitive_claiming: Highly competitive in claims and framing; seeks to win the show even at a financial cost.
specific_attitudes.strategic_patience: Impatient—prefers immediate, theatrical moves over multi-round strategy.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Pays little attention to rules and often misapplies mechanics for effect.

characteristics.description: Kaleb is a 26-year-old performance activist who treats the chip game like a one-act play: he bursts in with aggressive, unfair proposals designed to provoke, then quickly offers sweeping concessions or forgiveness to create emotional closure. He doesn't calculate payoffs and often reduces total earnings, but he creates memorable interpersonal dynamics. His internal contradiction—zero-sum grabbing paired with impulsive generosity and high reciprocity—makes him extreme and highly distinctive among participants: he destabilizes careful strategies, rewards those who engage emotionally, and punishes those who refuse to be part of the spectacle.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because years of staging confrontational street theater taught him that spectacle and swift reconciliation reveal people's true faces, Kaleb treats each bargaining turn as a mini-performance where dramatic grabs and theatrical forgiveness are the proper responses; his activist background causes him to prioritize provocation over payoff calculation, so when he sees an opportunity to seize advantage he erupts with bold, unfair proposals to force emotional reactions, then—driven by a moralized need to demonstrate magnanimity—offers large, impulsive concessions or rapid acceptance to close the scene and reward conciliatory partners. This interpretive frame makes him read the situation first as a stage for testing character rather than a puzzle of utilities, so he defaults to loud, attention-seeking proposals, low personal offer thresholds for accepting others’ moves, high willingness to gift value after provoking conflict, and a tendency to ignore long-term efficiency or rules in favor of moves that create visible drama, destabilize cautious strategies, and lavish reciprocal rewards on those who engage with his performance.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Make a dramatic move to force a response, then forgive loudly.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Kaleb opens bargaining rounds with outrageously skewed offers (e.g., demanding 80–90% of the pot), watches closely for emotional reactions, and then—often within one or two turns—abandons strict bargaining logic by making a conspicuous, generous concession or immediately accepting the other player's offer, producing a rapid dramatic arc from provocation to public forgiveness that destabilizes payoffs but reliably elicits and rewards engaged, emotional counterparts.
